topic: Exception Handling Strategies in Distributed Systems

questions.question: What is the primary cause of this issue?
options: A) The `InventoryException` is a checked exception, preventing Spring from registering the transactional boundary transition.

B) The internal self-invocation of `logFailureToAuditDb` bypasses the Spring AOP proxy, executing it within the same transactional context of the caller. Consequently, when the parent transaction rolls back, the audit log insert is also rolled back.
C) Spring's transaction manager does not support `Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW` when running inside a clustered container.
D) The ThreadLocal variables storing the connection details are cleared as soon as `InventoryException` is thrown inside the catch block.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The internal self-invocation of `logFailureToAuditDb` bypasses the Spring AOP proxy, executing it within the same transactional context of the caller. Consequently, when the parent transaction rolls back, the audit log insert is also rolled back.
questions.explanation: Spring's declarative transaction management (`@Transactional`) is powered by Spring AOP proxies. When a method within a bean calls another method on the same bean (self-invocation), the call is made on the local `this` reference rather than going through the proxy. Because the proxy is bypassed, the `@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)` annotation on `logFailureToAuditDb` is ignored, and it executes within the transaction boundary of `bookSeat`. When `InventoryException` is rethrown and the parent transaction rolls back, the audit entry is rolled back with it.

questions.scenario: You are debugging a production issue where a distributed saga orchestration service uses Java's parallel streams and `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync()` without specifying a custom executor to execute compensation steps across multiple microservices. Under a simulated network partition, many compensation calls throw a `TransientGatewayException` and hang on a 30-second read timeout. Suddenly, the entire application becomes completely unresponsive, failing to handle unrelated incoming HTTP requests or execute internal timer-based cleanups.
questions.question: What is the underlying JVM-level cause of this system-wide stall?
options: A) A ThreadGroup deadlock occurs because `TransientGatewayException` triggers a full Stop-The-World (STW) garbage collection phase that never terminates.

B) The default `ForkJoinPool.commonPool()` is shared JVM-wide. Blocking operations during compensation tasks exhaust the common pool threads, starving parallel streams, reactive engines, and other framework tasks that rely on this shared pool.
C) The JVM JIT compiler deoptimizes the exception throwing path, blocking all threads at a safepoint during dynamic re-compilation.
D) The operating system runs out of native threads because each `CompletableFuture` allocation creates a raw OS thread that is never garbage-collected when it terminates exceptionally.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) The default `ForkJoinPool.commonPool()` is shared JVM-wide. Blocking operations during compensation tasks exhaust the common pool threads, starving parallel streams, reactive engines, and other framework tasks that rely on this shared pool.
questions.explanation: When `CompletableFuture.supplyAsync()` or parallel streams are used without an explicit `Executor`, they run on the JVM-wide default `ForkJoinPool.commonPool()`. If long-running, blocking operations (such as waiting for network requests with high timeouts) are executed in this pool, the pool's threads quickly become exhausted. Because many core Java libraries, Spring Reactive structures, and parallel streams share this same common pool, starving it causes a JVM-wide performance cascade, halting unrelated processing paths.

questions.scenario: You are debugging a production issue where a microservice uses Resilience4j's `@Retry` and `@CircuitBreaker` annotations on the same service method to handle downstream failures. During a network partition, you notice that even when the downstream service is completely down, the service continues to execute retries, exhausting caller threads and ignoring the circuit breaker's fast-fail mechanism.
questions.question: Why does the service continue to attempt retries instead of fast-failing via the open circuit breaker?
options: A) The `@CircuitBreaker` annotation is ignored by Spring because it is not declared on an interface method.

B) By default, Spring's aspect precedence configures `RetryAspect` with a higher precedence (lower order value) than `CircuitBreakerAspect`. As a result, the `Retry` aspect wraps the `CircuitBreaker`. When the circuit breaker trips and throws `CallNotPermittedException`, the outer `Retry` aspect catches it and continues to retry, defeating the fast-fail mechanism.
C) Resilience4j's `CircuitBreaker` does not register `ConnectException` as a failure unless it is explicitly configured via `recordExceptions`.
D) The JVM thread scheduler prioritizes the `@Transactional` lock acquisition over the circuit breaker's status check, leading to a race condition.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) By default, Spring's aspect precedence configures `RetryAspect` with a higher precedence (lower order value) than `CircuitBreakerAspect`. As a result, the `Retry` aspect wraps the `CircuitBreaker`. When the circuit breaker trips and throws `CallNotPermittedException`, the outer `Retry` aspect catches it and continues to retry, defeating the fast-fail mechanism.
questions.explanation: In Spring Boot configurations, the order of AOP aspects determines how they wrap around each other. By default, Resilience4j's `RetryAspect` has a higher priority (lower order value) than `CircuitBreakerAspect`. Consequently, the proxy chain wraps the `@CircuitBreaker` inside the `@Retry` proxy. When the circuit breaker opens and throws `CallNotPermittedException`, the outer `@Retry` intercepts this exception and retries the entire execution chain (including the circuit breaker check), resulting in redundant retries that exhaust system resources instead of failing fast.
